etymology.description: semantic 酉(酉) (yǒu, "drink") + phonetic 將(將) (jiāng, "to lead/drop") -> sauce
etymology.interpretation: The character combines 酉(酉) (yǒu, "drink") as the semantic component indicating a liquid or fermented substance, with 將(將) (jiāng, "to lead/drop") as the phonetic component, which provides the sound. Together, they represent a fermented or preserved sauce used to enhance the flavor of food.
etymology.simplification: Simplified by replacing 將(將) (jiāng, "to lead/drop") with a simpler structure resembling 丬(丬) (pán, a radical suggesting plate or container) and 夕(夕) (xī, "evening"), reducing complexity.

examples.chinese: 这道菜需要一些辣酱(這道菜需要一些辣醬)。
examples.pinyin: Zhè dào cài xūyào yīxiē là jiàng.
examples.english: This dish needs some spicy sauce.

examples.chinese: 他喜欢把酱加在意大利面上(他喜歡把醬加在意大利麵上)。
examples.pinyin: Tā xǐhuān bǎ jiàng jiā zài Yìdàlì miàn shàng.
examples.english: He likes to add sauce on spaghetti.

examples.chinese: 我买了一瓶新酱(我買了一瓶新醬)，等不及想试试(等不及想試試)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ mǎile yī píng xīn jiàng, děng bù jí xiǎng shìshì.
examples.english: I bought a new bottle of sauce and can't wait to try it.
